Name: TrainingFreeLatencyAwareInference
Title: Training-Free Latency-Aware Adaptive Inference for Large Language Models
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training-free, latency-aware adaptive inference framework can dynamically adjust computational resources based on real-time activation geometry, ensuring strict wall-clock latency targets while maintaining high accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as dLLM-Cache, DAEDAL, and CadLLM have explored training-free methods to improve the efficiency and performance of large language models (LLMs) and diffusion-based LLMs (dLLMs). These methods focus on caching, variable-length denoising, and confidence-aware calibration to reduce inference latency. However, they often lack a unified framework that dynamically adjusts computational resources based on real-time activation geometry. Our proposal aims to fill this gap by introducing a novel, training-free, latency-aware adaptive inference framework that leverages spectral geometry to optimize resource allocation.
Abstract: We propose a training-free, latency-aware adaptive inference framework for large language models (LLMs) that dynamically adjusts computational resources based on real-time activation geometry. By computing local activation covariances and spectral condition numbers, our framework identifies tokens that are robust to low-precision computation and those that require higher precision. This allows for a precision-switching policy that enforces strict wall-clock latency targets without compromising accuracy. We theoretically ground our approach in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to existing static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.

Experiments.Name: Spectral Condition Number Analysis
Experiments.Description: Compute the local activation covariances and spectral condition numbers for a set of benchmark tasks and input distributions. Analyze the correlation between these metrics and the sensitivity of hidden states to quantization.
Experiments.Metrics: Spectral condition number, quantization sensitivity, and accuracy retention.

Experiments.Name: Precision-Switching Policy Evaluation
Experiments.Description: Implement a precision-switching policy based on the spectral condition number and evaluate its impact on inference latency and accuracy. Compare the results with static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.

Experiments.Name: Latency-Aware Dynamic Compute Throttling
Experiments.Description: Introduce a dynamic compute-throttling scheduler that adaptively routes tokens through fewer layers or prunes attention heads when subspace stability exceeds a calibrated threshold. Evaluate the performance under strict wall-clock latency targets.
Experiments.Metrics: Wall-clock latency, accuracy, and resource utilization.

Experiments.Name: Adversarial and Out-of-Distribution Robustness
Experiments.Description: Test the robustness of the proposed framework under adversarial attacks and out-of-distribution inputs. Measure the accuracy retention and computational efficiency.
Experiments.Metrics: Robustness to adversarial attacks, out-of-distribution accuracy, and computational efficiency.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The spectral condition number may not capture all aspects of activation geometry, potentially leading to suboptimal precision switching.
The dynamic compute-throttling scheduler could introduce additional overhead, affecting overall latency.
The framework's performance may vary across different model architectures and input distributions, requiring extensive benchmarking and validation.
